does: This paper introduces ASGuard, a mechanistically-informed defense framework that mitigates targeted jailbreaking attacks on large language models, specifically addressing vulnerabilities exploited by simple linguistic manipulations like tense-changing attacks. The approach operates in three stages: first, circuit analysis identifies specific attention heads causally responsible for the vulnerability; second, a channel-wise scaling vector is trained to recalibrate activations in these vulnerable heads; third, preventative fine-tuning forces the model to learn more robust refusal mechanisms. The method is evaluated across four LLMs, demonstrating significant reductions in attack success rates while maintaining general model capabilities and minimizing performance degradation.
connects: This work builds on growing understanding of brittleness in safety-aligned LLMs and the generalization gaps revealed by jailbreaking research, particularly work demonstrating that models refuse harmful requests inconsistently depending on superficial linguistic properties. It extends mechanistic interpretability approaches, specifically circuit analysis techniques that identify causally important components in neural networks, to the safety domain. The paper also connects to work on targeted model editing and surgical intervention methods that modify specific model components rather than retraining entire models, combining interpretability-driven identification of vulnerabilities with precision-targeted activation modification and fine-tuning strategies.
enables: ASGuard provides a foundation for more systematic, mechanistically-grounded approaches to LLM safety that go beyond broad alignment methods by targeting specific failure modes. This enables future work to identify and patch other targeted vulnerabilities through circuit analysis, potentially creating a library of attack-specific defenses. The framework suggests pathways toward more robust alignment methods that understand and address the underlying mechanisms of refusal behaviors rather than relying on brittle surface-level patterns. It also points toward hybrid defense strategies combining interpretability-guided identification with surgical intervention, potentially scaling to address multiple vulnerability classes and enabling more resilient deployment of LLMs in safety-critical applications.
